sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=20, col=24), RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 9, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves active object right 2px within container (VERIFIED step 3)', 'LEFT': 'Moves active object left 2px within container (VERIFIED step 4)', 'UP': 'Swaps active object with nearest divider block above at same row level (VERIFIED step 5)', 'DOWN': 'Swaps active object back with divider block, reversing UP (VERIFIED step 6)', 'MOUSE': 'Clicking selects objects but only advances timer; may be needed to change which object is active (HYPOTHESIS)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'divider_blocks': 'White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, can be swapped with objects via UP/DOWN', 'colored_pixels': 'Small 2x2 movable objects inside containers on left panel', 'gray_containers': '4x8 gray boxes on left that hold colored pixels', 'white_containers': 'White frames on right panel containing colored shapes (red and blue)', 'blue_square': '2x4 blue block on left panel', 'red_blocks': 'Red and dark red blocks on left panel', 'pixel_pattern': 'Scattered blue/charcoal 1px objects forming a pattern on left'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_bar': 'White horizontal bar at row 63, shrinks as timer/progress indicator', 'divider': 'Vertical line of white 2x2 blocks at col 32-33, rows 10-50'}
Current level:
- World model: Two-panel layout: left (charcoal bg) has colored objects in gray containers; right (black bg) has colored objects in white containers. Vertical white divider at col 32-33. Arrow keys move active object: LEFT/RIGHT within container, UP/DOWN swaps with divider blocks. Green pixel (node 34) is currently the active object, restored to original position after DOWN.
- Goal model: Move colored objects from left containers through divider to right side to match targets in white containers. Need to figure out how to select different objects.
- Recent findings: ['UP swaps active object with divider block above', 'DOWN reverses the swap', 'LEFT/RIGHT move within container bounds', 'Node IDs track objects across swaps - colors move with nodes']
- Open questions: ["How to select/activate different objects? (MOUSE doesn't seem to change selection)", 'What is the target configuration on the right side?', 'Does UP always go to the nearest divider block, or a specific one?', 'Can objects be moved from right side back to left?']
- Plan: Try clicking on different objects to see if selection changes, then use arrow keys to move them. Need to move all left-side objects to right-side containers.
- Failed probes: ['MOUSE(20,24) on yellow pixel: only advanced timer, no gameplay change (step 1)']
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. Assistant text is optional; if you write any, keep it short and prefix lines with `Action semantics:`, `Object taxonomy:`, `HUD map:`, `Win pattern:`, `Level log:`, `Cross-level notes:`, `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, or `Failed probes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

Level 1: you have used 8 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After clicking on the yellow pixel and pressing RIGHT:
- Yellow stayed at [[20,24],[21,25]] - didn't move
- Green moved from [[40,10],[41,11]] to [[40,12],[41,13]] - moved right by 2 pixels
So clicking on the yellow pixel did NOT change the active object - the green pixel is still the active one! The green pixel moved right again.
This means MOUSE doesn't change selection. The active object seems to always be the green pixel (node 34).
Wait, but that doesn't make sense for a puzzle game. Let me think about this differently.
Maybe the active object is always the one that's "selected" by some other mechanism. Or maybe the game has a single active object that cycles through different objects with some action.
Let me check: maybe UP/DOWN cycles through objects rather than just swapping with divider blocks? Let me think about what happened:
1. Initial state: green pixel at [[40,10],[41,11]]
2. After RIGHT: green at [[40,12],[41,13]]
3. After LEFT: green at [[40,10],[41,11]] (back)
4. After UP: green swapped with divider block - green went to [[38,32],[39,33]], white went to [[38,10],[39,11]]
5. After DOWN: green back at [[40,10],[41,11]], white back at [[38,32],[39,33]]
6. After MOUSE on yellow: no change to objects
7. After RIGHT: green at [[40,12],[41,13]] again
So the active object is always the green pixel. MOUSE doesn't change selection.
Hmm, but then how do I move other objects? Maybe I need to first move the green pixel to where I want it, and then the selection changes? Or maybe the game works differently than I thought.
Wait, let me reconsider. Maybe the green pixel IS the player character/avatar, and the goal is to move it around to interact with other objects. Like, the green pixel is the "cursor" or "player" that moves things.
Let me think about what the game might be:
- Left side has objects in containers
- Right side has target configurations
- The green pixel is the player that needs to move objects from left to right
But the green pixel is inside a gray container. When I press UP, it swaps with a divider block. This moves it to the divider.
Maybe the mechanic is:
1. Move the green pixel (player) to a position adjacent to an object you want to move
2. Then some action picks up/moves that object
3. Then move the green pixel to the right side and place the object there
But that seems complicated. Let me try a different approach.
Let me check if the green pixel can move to other positions. Currently it's at [[40,12],[41,13]] (after the last RIGHT). Let me try more RIGHT to see if it can exit the container, or try UP/DOWN to see what happens from this position.
Actually wait - the green pixel is at [[40,12],[41,13]] now, which is still inside the gray container (node 32). The container boundary changed to accommodate it. Let me try RIGHT again to see if it can move further, or if it's at the edge.
Actually, I should first understand the container better. The gray container (node 32) was originally at [[38,8],[43,13]] - a 6x6 box. The green pixel was inside at [[40,10],[41,11]]. After RIGHT, it moved to [[40,12],[41,13]]. The right edge of the container is col 13, so the green pixel is now at the right edge.
Let me try RIGHT again to see if it can exit, or try UP to see what happens from this position.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole game. Maybe the goal isn't to move objects between panels. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me look at the right side more carefully. There are two white containers:
- Top one: white frame with red inside
- Bottom one: white frame with blue inside
These look like "target" configurations. The left side has various colored objects that might need to be arranged to match.
But the left side has:
- A gray container with yellow and blue inside
- A red block and dark red block
- A blue/charcoal pixel pattern
- A gray container with green inside
Maybe the goal is to rearrange the left side to match some pattern, or to move objects from left to right.
Let me try a different approach. Let me move the green pixel UP to the divider and see what happens next. Maybe once it's on the divider, it can move to the right side.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Green pixel is still the active object - MOUSE didn't change selection
# Green is at [[40,12],[41,13]] after RIGHT
# Let me try RIGHT again to see if it can exit container, then UP to divider

action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"After RIGHT: {last_action_result}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = {n['id']: n for n in seg['nodes']}

for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['id'] == 32:
        print(f"Container 32: boundary={n['boundary']} children={n.get('children')}")

# Try RIGHT again
action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"\nAfter 2nd RIGHT: {last_action_result}")

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['id'] == 32:
        print(f"Container 32: boundary={n['boundary']} children={n.get('children')}")

# Try UP from here
action(['UP'])
print(f"\nAfter UP: {last_action_result}")

seg3 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg3['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'W' and n['pixels'] == 4:
        if 30 <= n['boundary'][0][0] <= 45:
            print(f"Divider block: id={n['id']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>
{"game_tier": {"action_semantics": {"RIGHT": "Moves active object (green pixel) right 2px within container (VERIFIED)", "LEFT": "Moves active object left 2px within container (VERIFIED)", "UP": "Swaps active object with nearest divider block above (VERIFIED)", "DOWN": "Swaps active object with divider block below (VERIFIED)", "MOUSE": "Does NOT change active object selection (VERIFIED step 9)"}, "object_taxonomy": {"green_pixel": "Active/player object (node 34), 2x2", "divider_blocks": "White 2x2 blocks at col 32-33", "containers": "Gray boxes on left holding colored pixels"}, "hud_map": {"bottom_bar": "White bar at row 63"}, "win_pattern": {}, "level_log": {}, "cross_level_notes": {}}, "level_tier": {"world_model": "Green pixel (node 34) is always the active object. Arrow keys move it. UP/DOWN swap with divider blocks. MOUSE does not change selection.", "goal_model": "Unclear - need to understand what completing the level requires", "recent_findings": ["MOUSE does not change active object", "Green pixel is the only movable object"], "open_questions": ["What is the goal?", "Can green pixel move to right side?", "How do other objects get moved?"], "current_plan": "Try moving green pixel to divider, then see if it can go to right side", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["MOUSE on yellow pixel - no selection change"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
